title: The Mismatch Between Mutual Fund Scale and Skill
abstract: I demonstrate that skill and scale are mismatched among actively managed equity mutual funds. Many mutual fund investors confuse the effects of fund exposures to common systematic factors with managerial skill when allocating capital among funds. Active mutual funds with positive factor-related past returns thus accumulate assets to the point that they significantly underperform. I also show that the negative aggregate benchmark-adjusted performance of active equity mutual funds is driven mainly by these oversized funds.

article.introduction:
The extent to which anactively managed mutual fund can add value for investors depends not only on the portfolio manager's skill in discovering superior investment opportunities but also on the scale of the fund. Academic research over the past several decades shows that the aggregate active equity mutual fund portfolio has significantly negative benchmark-adjusted returns (alphas) after fees1and that the performance of an average fund is not persistent over time. The seminal work of Berk and Green (2004) (BG) points out that the lack of return persistence is consistent with a model of rational investors competing for scarce skill and active funds subject to decreasing returns to scale. However, the BG theory also predicts that managerial skill is matched with fund scale so that mutual funds earn zero expected alpha net of fees, which is hard to reconcile with the negative aggregate after-fee performance.
An important assumption of BG is that mutual fund investors rationally evaluate managerial skill and allocate capital accordingly. However, this assumption seems to be at odds with the characteristics of mutual fund investors and with previous evidence about their behavior.2According to the 2011Investment Company Institute (ICI) Fact Book, for example, 93.7% of mutual fund assets in the United States were held by households. If mutual fund investors are not as sophisticated in assessing skill as modeled by BG, then certain funds would receive more assets than justified by their portfolio managers' skill, and as a result observe negative performance due to diminishing returns to scale,3whereas other funds might become too small. That is, in a market in which investors do not correctly evaluate managerial skill, the deviation of actual fund size from fund capacity (the equilibrium size conjectured by BG) would be a key predictor of future performance.
In this paper, I demonstrate that skill and scale are indeed significantly mismatched among actively managed equity mutual funds. In particular, because many mutual fund investors do not adjust for common factors such as the Fama–French size and value factors when allocating capital among funds, actively managed funds with positive prior factor-related returns (FRRs) accumulate assets to the point that they significantly underperform various benchmarks in the future. In this sense, fund flows associated with FRRs are excessive and cannot be justified by managerial skill. I further show that excess fund size, rather than total fund size, significantly predicts future performance: controlling for fund size, mutual funds that have attracted flows through factor exposures significantly underperform benchmarks and other funds of the same size.
My analysis builds on the observation of Berk and van Binsbergen (2016) and Barber, Huang, and Odean (2016) that mutual fund flows respond positively to fund past returns arising from exposures to common factors other than the market factor.4In a fully rational world, mutual fund investors would distinguish return components due to managerial skill, such as processing private information and discovering mispriced stocks, from components due to factor exposures (Grinblatt and Titman (1989) and Pástor and Stambaugh (2002a)). Due to actual investor behavior, however, actively managed funds with positive prior FRRs accumulate so many assets that they have negative expected alphas in the future. I also show that the negative net alphas of the aggregate mutual fund portfolio documented by, for example, Fama and French (2010), are driven mainly by the poor performance of the small set of oversized funds that have significantly positive prior FRRs.
My empirical study relies on the CRSP mutual fund database. I focus on actively managed equity mutual funds. To estimate a fund's FRR, I follow Barber, Huang, and Odean (2016) and use a seven-factor model that augments the Fama–French–Carhart (FFC) four-factor model with the three industry factors5of Pástor and Stambaugh (2002b) as the baseline model. The FRR is calculated as the sum of the return components that are traced to size, value, momentum, and the three industry factors. For robustness, in AppendixAI use the FFC four-factor model to estimate factor-related returns and I obtain similar results.
I start by showing that fund flows respond positively to FRRs. Controlling for factor-adjusted expected returns (the seven-factor alpha), I find that fund flows are positively correlated with FRRs, regardless of whether flows are measured in absolute dollars or as a proportion of fund's total assets under management (AUM). For example, funds whose average FRR over the previous four years is in the top tercile of the sample distribution receive three times as much flows per quarter, on average, as other funds with the same factor-adjusted expected returns. This evidence is consistent with the findings of Berk and van Binsbergen (2016) and Barber, Huang, and Odean (2016).
I next demonstrate that fund flows associated with positive FRRs lead to negative fund future performance. To this end, I first control for fund AUM. I find that funds that reach the current size because of positive prior FRRs significantly underperform various benchmarks and other funds, despite having similar AUM. For example, within each AUM quintile group, funds with top-tercile past FRRs significantly underperform bottom-tercile-FRR funds by around 300 to 400 bps over the next year, depending on the benchmark. Across the five AUM quintiles, funds with top-tercile past FRRs have average negative future alphas of−230to−250bps per year. Funds with middle-tercile FRRs have net alphas of about−20bps, while funds with bottom-tercile FRRs have net average alphas of around 70 to 80 bps. These results indicate that how a fund grows its AUM, rather than fund AUM alone, determines fund future performance.
I further show that this sharp performance difference is not due to the difference in prior “real” alphas. Specifically, I find that controlling for the prior seven-factor alpha, funds that attract additional flows through positive FRRs still underperform other funds in the future. In a comprehensive set of robustness checks, I rule out several alternative explanations. In sum, the findings are consistent with a market in which active funds with positive prior FRRs accumulate so many assets that they subsequently observe significantly negative performance due to diminishing returns to scale.
In additional analysis, I show that the negative performance of funds with high prior FRRs can be explained by FRR-driven flows through the scale effect. Previous work, for example, Chen et al. (2004), Pollet and Wilson (2008), Edelen, Evans, and Kadlec (2013), and Pástor, Stambaugh, and Taylor (2018), identifies trading costs such as price impact and execution costs as important drivers of active funds' diminishing returns to scale. I measure mutual fund trading costs following Pástor, Stambaugh, and Taylor (2018). By exploring cross-sectional variation in proportional trading costs, I find that the negative performance of active funds with positive prior FRRs is more significant among those funds that have higher trading costs. By comparison, trading costs have no predictive power for future return performance among those funds that have nonpositive FRRs. These findings further support the view of excessive flows combined with scale effect.
In addition to this fund-level scale effect, I find that funds with high prior FRRs are positively exposed to styles that have experienced large aggregate mutual fund buying driven by uninformative fund flows. These “crowded” styles have large negative subsequent returns, so that performance of high-FRR funds is also negatively affected. Specifically, I construct 27 (3×3×3) style portfolios each year by sorting stocks by size, book-to-market ratio, and past one-year return. I then use a bottom-up approach to measure uninformative flow-induced trading at the style level. I find that even after controlling for the FFC factors, the top-third “crowded” styles observe abnormal returns of about−4%per year as nonfundamental price pressure dissipates eventually, while the one-third of styles with the most flow-induced selling observe significantly positive future alphas. This flow-driven style effect explains around 20% to25%of the negative performance of funds with positive FRRs.
This paper contributes to the extensive mutual fund literature in several ways. First, building on the observation that mutual fund flows do not account for systematic factors, this paper shows that the flows from investors based on factor-related past returns are greater than justified by managerial skill and lead to significant negative future performance. This is the first paper to point out that mutual fund skill and scale are significantly mismatched due to a systematic mistake that many mutual fund investors make. Although previous work relates total fund size and performance,6I show that when investors do not correctly evaluate managerial skill, excess fund size largely determines fund future performance. Earlier work also examines the response of fund flows to past fund performance.7This paper goes further by highlighting the performance implications of different flow components.
The results in this paper also contribute to our understanding of mutual fund performance. Previous work such as Fama and French (2010) documents that the aggregate actively managed equity mutual fund portfolio has significantly negative alphas after fees and near-zero average alphas before fees. Based on this observation, previous studies argue that equity fund managers lack skill. I show instead that the negative aggregate return performance is explained mainly by the poor performance of the small set of active funds that observed strong prior FRRs and thus have become too large relative to their managerial skill.
In contemporaneous study, Müller, Rosenberger, and Uhrig-Homburg (2018) take a theoretical perspective that is closely related to the motivation of this paper. In particular, the authors argue that when confusing a mutual fund's “fake” alpha with its “true” alpha, investors may overinvest in the fund. Müller, Rosenberger, and Uhrig-Homburg (2018) document two key empirical findings. First, they find a negative relationship between fund returns and the deviation of fund size from its time-series average. Second, for funds with an insignificant 24-month rolling-window correlation between a fund's age and the fund's assets, they find a negative average net alpha relative to the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM). My results are distinct from those of Müller, Rosenberger, and Uhrig-Homburg (2018) in that I show that fund flows associated with past factor-related returns lead to negative subsequent fund returns largely through the effect of decreasing returns to scale.
The rest of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIintroduces the data set and the econometric models used in this paper. SectionIIprovides evidence on how mutual fund flows respond to fund returns that are related to common factors beyond the aggregate market. SectionIIIshows that mutual funds that experience different factor-related returns perform differently in the future. SectionIVpresents additional evidence that is consistent with the view of excessive flows combined with decreasing returns to scale. SectionVhighlights the implications for aggregate mutual fund performance and quantifies the dollar costs for mutual fund investors that chase factor-related returns. SectionVIconcludes. Robustness checks and additional results are reported in AppendicesAandB.
article.I. Data and Factor Models:
In this section, I describe the mutual fund data set and the procedure I follow to estimate factor-related returns, factor-adjusted expected returns, and fund flows, the three key variables of interest in this paper.
Sample funds come from the standard CRSP survivorship-bias-free mutual fund database over the period 1984 to 2014. I restrict the sample to mutual funds that start in 1984 or later because the earlier data are not reliable (Fama and French (2010)). I focus on actively managed equity mutual funds that invest most of their assets in the domestic equity market (CRSP Objective Code starting with “ED”).
For the purpose of this analysis, I eliminate index funds, balanced funds, sector funds, and exchange-traded funds (ETFs). Mutual funds often market different share classes of the same portfolio to different types of clients. The key difference across these subclasses is typically the fee structure. I aggregate all subclasses into a single fund. I include only those funds whose monthly returns are available for at least four years. I then exclude funds that never managed more than $5 million in total assets. The resulting sample comprises more than 4,000 distinct mutual funds.
A mutual fund's expected return can be decomposed using linear factor models into the fund's factor-adjusted expected return and the mean return components arising from exposures to the specified set of factors. For this decomposition, I use a seven-factor model of fund returns as in Barber, Huang, and Odean (2016). The seven-factor model includes the four factors of the FFC model in Carhart (1997) and the three industry factors of Pástor and Stambaugh (2002b).
I include the three industry factors when estimating FRRs for two reasons. First, Barber, Huang, and Odean (2016) show that fund exposures to the three industry factors have significant predictive power for mutual fund flows. Second, the three industry factors explain a substantial portion of cross-sectional variation in mutual fund returns (Pástor and Stambaugh (2002b)). In a similar exercise, I also calculate factor-related average returns relying on the FFC four-factor model. Results based on the four-factor model, reported in AppendixA, are similar.
article.II. Factor-Related Returns and Fund Flow:
In this section, I examine how mutual fund flows respond to FRRs. I find that controlling for factor-adjusted expected returns (“real” alpha), mutual fund flows are positively correlated with FRRs, consistent with the findings of Berk and van Binsbergen (2016) and Barber, Huang, and Odean (2016). After establishing these flow patterns, in SectionIII, I estimate the effect of a mutual fund's past FRRs on its future returns.
I take a rolling-window approach, as follows. For each calendar year of data, I sort all mutual funds into quintile portfolios based on a fund's factor-adjusted expected return in (1), as estimated using monthly returns over the prior four years. I then split each quintile into three subgroups based on the sample distribution of FRRs in (2) across all mutual funds, as estimated over the same four-year period. Thus, the first group within each quintile consists of funds whose estimated factor-related average past four-year return is in the top third of all funds by that measure (not just the set of funds included in that quintile). Similarly, the middle (bottom) group comprises mutual funds whose estimated factor-related average past four-year return is in the middle (bottom) third of all funds by that measure.9Specifically, Kamstra et al. (2017) find that flows of investment into equity mutual funds from other fund categories, such as money market and bond mutual funds, are more likely in the spring. To account for this seasonality in asset allocation, each of the four-year estimation windows ends in March.10
I next compare average quarterly flows over a given four-year period into the three groups of funds within a seven-factor-alpha quintile. This analysis sheds light on how mutual fund flows respond to FRRs, after controlling for factor-adjusted expected returns. TableIreports the time-series average of fund flows as well as the time-series average of FRRs for all fund portfolios.
As one can see, the first group of funds in each seven-factor-alpha quintile has positive average FRRs (ranging from 3.6% to 5.0% per year). On average, these funds attract more flows than the two lower FRR groups within a given quintile. For the middle group in each quintile, average FRRs are near0%and average flows are higher than those of the bottom group, which has negative average FRRs. Due to these differences in fund flows, on average the top FRR group in each seven-factor-alpha quintile has larger AUM at the end of a given four-year estimation period than the two lower FRR groups in the same quintile. By comparing the rows in columns (3), (8), or (13) of TableI, one can see that fund flows also respond positively to factor-adjusted expected returns.
The last column of TableIreports standard errors of tests of a positive difference in average fund flows between the top and bottom groups within a given seven-factor-alpha quintile. To estimate the standard errors for the flow differences, I use the time-series moving-block bootstrap approach of Kunsch (1989). This approach accounts for serial and cross-sectional dependency in fund flows.11I bootstrap the sample 5,000 times. The standard errors are estimated as the sample standard deviations of the corresponding flow differences across all bootstrap samples. As one can see, all of the test statistics are significant, implying a rejection of the null hypothesis of identical average flows into the top and bottom groups of each of the factor-adjusted return quintiles.
In summary, TableIshows that higher past FRRs are associated with larger fund flows. Given that fund flows reveal investors' beliefs of managerial skill, this result is consistent with an economy in which many mutual fund investors treat return components attributable to size, value, momentum, and the industry factors as though they are signals of active management skill and in which these investors respond by investing more in funds with higher prior FRRs.
article.III. Past Factor-Related Returns and Fut:
SectionIIshows that FRRs are positively correlated with mutual fund flows. In a fully rational world, however, mutual fund investors should be able to distinguish performance that is a result of managerial skill from performance that is a result of exposures to systematic factors, regardless of whether the factors are priced (Grinblatt and Titman (1989) and Pástor and Stambaugh (2002a)). In this section, I show that fund flows associated with positive FRRs lead to negative fund future performance. In this sense, flows into funds with positive prior FRRs are greater than justified by the fund managers' skill. I provide additional evidence in support of excessive flows combined with decreasing returns to scale in SectionIV.
As pointed out by Berk and Green (2004) and empirically supported by, for example, Chen et al. (2004), Edelen, Evans, and Kadlec (2013), Harvey and Liu (2017), and Zhu (2018), the more assets that an active fund manages, the worse is the fund's return performance. Because mutual fund investors confuse FRRs with managerial skill, funds with positive prior FRRs are likely to have more AUM than they can manage and thus observe negative expected future performance. To illustrate, consider two mutual funds that have the same AUM at a given point in time, but Fund A has reached its current size due to positive prior FRRs while Fund B has negligible FRRs and has instead attracted flows by relying on its “real” alpha. Fund A would be expected to have worse future return performance than Fund B, despite the fact that they have similar size.
To provide evidence on this phenomenon, I use a rolling-window approach, as follows. For each calendar year of data, I sort all mutual funds into quintile portfolios based on fund AUM.12I then split each AUM quintile into three subgroups depending on the sample distribution of FRRs over the past four years as in SectionII. The top-FRR subgroups have average FRRs of about 400 bps while the bottom-FRR subgroups have negative average FRRs of−300bps. To capture future return performance, I compute the monthly AUM-weighted returns over the next one year for each fund group. The time series of the monthly AUM-weighted returns for a given fund portfolio is then benchmarked to the FFC four-factor model and the seven-factor model in (1). The results are presented in TableII.
Some striking patterns can be seen. Within each AUM quintile, that is, controlling for fund AUM, funds with higher prior FRRs perform worse in the future. For example, when benchmarked to the FFC four-factor model, future net alphas of top-tercile funds by prior FRRs are 286 bps to 396 bps lower per year than those of the bottom-tercile funds with similar AUM. When benchmarked to the seven-factor model, the differences range between 319 bps and 418 bps.
Across the five AUM quintiles, funds with top-tercile prior FRRs have average net alphas of −235 bps to −253 bps (significant at 1% level) over the next year depending on the benchmark. In contrast, funds with lowest tercile prior FRRs have, on average, positive net alphas of 67 bps and 78 bps under the two benchmark models. The middle-tercile funds have average net alphas of around −20 bps.
In AppendixA, I show that the results are robust to excluding the National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER) recessions and to using alternative rolling windows and different factor models. The results also hold when examining CRSP-classified cap-based funds and style-based funds separately. In summary, because fund flows do not differentiate between factor exposures and managerial skill, how a fund grows its AUM significantly determines its future performance.
In SectionsIIandIII.A, I show that(i)mutual fund flows chase positive FRRs and,(ii)controlling for AUM, funds that attract flows through FRRs significantly underperform other funds in the future. In this section, I show that the underperformance associated with FRR-driven flows is not due to prior differences in “real” alphas, as funds with positive prior FRRs continue to significantly underperform other funds after controlling for past factor-adjusted expected returns.
To this end, here I follow the rolling-window procedure of SectionIII.A, but I sort all funds into quintile portfolios based on the prior-year factor-adjusted expected returns in (1). As in SectionIII.A, I split each quintile into three groups based on FRRs over the past four years, and I measure the future performance of each fund portfolio relative to the three benchmark models. The results are reported in TableIII.
As one can see, controlling for past factor-adjusted expected returns (i.e.,“real” alphas), funds with higher prior FRRs still underperform benchmarks and other funds. For example, when benchmarked to the FFC model, funds with top-tercile FRRs underperform bottom-FRR funds by 222 bps to 417 bps across the five quintiles. In TableA.III, I show that positive prior FRRs continue to predict negative future performance after controlling for AUM and “real” alpha simultaneously. AppendixAalso shows that similar results obtain when including dollar value added (DVA) as a control for managerial skill (Berk and van Binsbergen,2015).
A superficially plausible alternative story is that the negative performance of funds with positive prior FRRs is due to mean reversion in factor exposures. Indeed, Barber, Huang, and Odean (2016) find that mutual fund exposures to those factors show little persistence. This alternative story does not explain the evidence, however, as I measure fund future performance relative to both the FFC four-factor model and the seven-factor model, which strip out the impact of mean reversion in factor exposures.
Another observation from TablesIIandIIIis that the negative aggregate performance of mutual funds as documented by Fama and French (2010) is driven mainly by the poor performance of the top-tercile funds by prior FRRs. I provide more detailed analysis on this result in SectionV. I also quantify the dollar costs for mutual fund investors that chase past FRRs.
In summary, the results in SectionsII.,III., and AppendixAprovide consistent evidence in line with a market in which mutual funds with positive prior FRRs accumulate excessive AUM, which leads to diminishing returns to scale and in turn significantly negative future return performance. In other words, these funds receive flows greater than their managerial skill justifies. I provide more evidence in support of excessive flows combined with diminishing returns to scale next.
article.IV. Causes of Underperformance with Posi:
In this section, I provide additional evidence that fund flows associated with FRRs, through the effect of diminishing returns to scale, explain a large part of the negative future performance of funds with positive FRRs. Beyond this fund-level scale effect, I show that these high-FRR funds are positively exposed to styles that have had large aggregate mutual fund buying that is induced by uninformative fund flows. As these “crowded” styles observe large negative subsequent returns, the performance of high-FRR funds is also negatively affected.
Previous studies (e.g., Pollet and Wilson (2008), Edelen, Evans, and Kadlec (2013), Pástor, Stambaugh, and Taylor (2018)) identify trading costs such as price impact and execution costs as important causes of diminishing returns to scale for actively managed equity mutual funds.13If fund flows associated with FRRs, through the scale effect, explain the negative future performance of funds with positive prior FRRs, one would expect a stronger scale effect among those funds that have higher trading costs. I test this hypothesis by exploring the cross-sectional variation in the trading costs of active mutual funds.
With the characterization of mutual funds' trading costs, the exercise proceeds as follows.14For each calendar year of data, I sort the top tercile of funds by FRRs over the past four years into three subgroups based on the expected average proportional trading costs in (9) over the next year. For this purpose, I do not need to specify the paramterc∼. For comparison, I also sort the other two-thirds of funds (those with lower FRRs) into tercile portfolios based on the same measure of trading costs. I study future performance for all six fund portfolios over the next year as in SectionIII.A.
TableIVpresents the results. In the top-tercile group by prior FRRs, funds that face higher trading costs do indeed have more negative future return performance, consistent with excessive flows chasing FRRs combined with a scale effect. For example, the return spreads between the low-trading-cost and high-trading-cost funds are about 180 bps per year and are significant at the 1% level. By comparison, among the other two-thirds of funds (those with lower prior FRRs), the three trading-cost subgroups have similar benchmark-adjusted future returns, which are not statistically different from zero or from each other. In this sense, these funds, which attract flows based on their “real” alpha, are not oversized and thus trading costs have no predictive power for their future return performance.
To put the implied variation in trading costs into perspective, in analysis based on transaction-level data, Edelen, Evans, and Kadlec (2009,2013) and Busse et al. (2018) estimate large variation in mutual fund trading costs both across and within styles.15For example, Edelen, Evans, and Kadlec (2013) document average trading costs of around 80 bps for large-cap styles to 200 bps to 300 bps for small-cap styles. They also estimate the within-style standard deviation of trading costs to be 40 bps to 50 bps among large-cap styles, 60 bps to 100 bps among mid-cap styles, and 110 bps to 150 bps among small-cap styles. Busse et al. (2018) estimate the standard deviation of trading costs across all funds to be 135 bps. They also find that the within-style standard deviation ranges from 90 bps for large-cap styles to 200 bps for small-cap styles.16These estimates are largely in line with the implied variation in trading costs reported in TableIV.
To further validate the role of trading costs in driving the negative returns of “oversized” funds, I explore the variation in mutual fund trading costs across styles. Early work, for example, Chen et al. (2004), Yan (2008), and Edelen, Evans, and Kadlec (2013), shows that small-cap funds and growth funds tend to have higher average trading costs and a larger standard deviation in trading costs. In TableA.IV, I show that among these oversized funds, small-cap funds and growth funds do indeed have more negative average abnormal returns and higher variation in returns when sorted by trading costs. In summary, the evidence in this section and in AppendixAfurther supports the view that fund flows associated with FRRs are excessive and explain negative future performance through diminishing returns to scale.
That mutual fund investors do not account for factor exposures implies commonality in fund flows. Imagine, for example, that certain stock styles had high relative returns. Because mutual funds largely scale up or liquidate existing positions proportionally in response to fund flows,17these styles are likely to have attracted large uninformative flow-induced buying and thus have negative performance in the future as nonfundamental price impact eventually dissipates. Conversely, styles that have had large uninformative flow-induced mutual fund selling are likely to deliver positive future performance.18Intuitively, funds with high past FRRs are likely to be exposed to these “crowded” styles, so that their performance is negatively affected.
In this section, I use a bottom-up approach to measure uninformative flow-induced trading at the style level.19I find that styles that have experienced large flow-induced buying over the past few years generate future abnormal returns of about−4%to−5%per year on average, while styles with large flow-induced selling have positive future alphas. I then confirm that mutual funds with positive FRRs are positively exposed to the “crowded” styles. This flow-driven effect on style returns explains about 20% to 25% of the negative return performance documented in SectionIII.
To see the impact of mutual fund flow-induced trading on style returns, I sort all styles into three groups based onFITπ,tin (10) and I calculate the equal-weighted return for each style group over the next year. The time series of the equal-weighted style returns is then benchmarked to the FFC factors and the industry factors. The results are reported in TableV. In AppendixB, I split the 27 styles into finer groups. TableB.IIreports the results.
Consistent with the intuition, styles that have experienced large flow-induced buying observe negative abnormal returns of about−4%to−5%per year, while styles that have experienced large outflows observe positive alphas in the future. The spreads in alphas between these extreme styles are about7%per year and are significant at the 1% level. TableB.Ishows that this return pattern holds when excluding NBER recessions. TableB.IIIfurther confirms the negative relationship between flow-induced trading and future style returns using panel regressions.
I next confirm that funds with high past FRRs are positively exposed to styles that have had large uninformed flow-induced trading. To do so, I regress returns of the top-tercile-FRR funds on returns of the high flow-induced trading (FIT) styles and returns of the low-FIT styles, controlling for the seven factors. TableVIreports the results. The high-FRR funds are indeed positively exposed to the “crowded” styles and negatively exposed to the low-FIT styles. Funds with negative FRRs show the opposite while funds with middle-tercile FRRs are not exposed to the high-FIT and low-FIT styles.
TableVIalso shows the extent to which the performance of funds with positive prior FRRs is affected by this flow-driven style effect. In SectionIII, I show that the top-tercile funds by past FRRs earn average net alphas of about −230 bps to −250 bps depending on the benchmark. One can see from TableVIthat about 55 bps of the negative alphas is due to fund exposure to the high-FIT styles. In other words, the flow-driven effect on style returns can explain around 20% to 25% of the negative performance of the high-FRR funds.
article.V. Implications for Aggregate Fund Perfo:
In this section, I demonstrate that the negative aggregate performance of actively managed mutual funds is driven mainly by the poor performance of the small set of funds with positive prior FRRs. By not accounting for factor exposures, investors lost around $15 billion per year on average over the sample period.
Malkiel (1995), Gruber (1996), Wermers (2000), and Fama and French (2010) document that the aggregate actively managed equity mutual fund portfolio observes significantly negative benchmark-adjusted returns after accounting for management fees. Based on this observation, researches have argued that mutual fund managers lack skill. Evidence in TablesIIandIIIsuggests that this negative aggregate performance is driven mainly by the poor performance of the top-tercile funds by prior FRRs, which have become too large relative to their managerial skill. The majority of mutual funds, however, are not oversized and thus have nonnegative net alphas.
To show this more clearly, for each calendar year of data, I split all funds into two groups. The first group consists of the top third of funds sorted by past-four-year FRRs in (2). The second group is the remainder of funds. I measure the future performance of each group relative to five benchmark models as in SectionIII.A. These benchmark models are as follows: the CAPM model, the FFC four-factor model, the seven-factor model in (1), a nine-factor model that adds the profitability and investment factors of Fama and French (2015) to the seven-factor model, and the Vanguard benchmark model as proposed by Berk and van Binsbergen (2015), which uses the monthly returns of the Vanguard index funds as the benchmark returns.22
From Panel A of TableVII, the aggregate AUM-weighted mutual fund portfolio has significantly negative net alphas of −81 bps to −123 bps per year under the five benchmark models. These magnitudes are similar to those documented in Fama and French (2010). The average benchmark-adjusted net returns of the top-tercile funds by past FRRs range from −182 bps to −253 bps per year. By comparison, the remaining two-thirds of funds have net alphas that range from−4bpsto23bpsunder different benchmark models and are not statistically different from zero.
Panel B shows return performance gross of fees. The aggregate mutual fund portfolio earns near-zero benchmark-adjusted gross returns, similar to those in Fama and French (2010). In particular, the two-thirds of funds with lower past FRRs have significantly positive gross alphas of 97 bps to 128 bps per year, indicating that they are sufficiently skilled to outperform those benchmarks before fees.
Panel C presents the average dollar profit and loss of mutual fund investment to investors. Under the five benchmarks, investors lost around $10 to $15 billion per year over the past 30 years. The losses were mostly from the oversized funds with high past FRRs.
Berk and van Binsbergen (2016) argue that the CAPM model is the closest to the “true” asset pricing model that investors use. However, the findings in TableVIIare hard to reconcile with a theory under which mutual fund investors are rational and CAPM is the “true” asset pricing model. By chasing funds with a high CAPM alpha that is due to FRRs, many mutual fund investors earn a significantly negative CAPM alpha in the future. Those investors, if they were rational and cared about market-adjusted returns, should not earn a negative CAPM alpha.
article.VI. Conclusion:
In this paper, I demonstrate that skill and scale are mismatched among actively managed equity mutual funds. Mutual fund flows respond positively to returns associated with exposures to common factors like the Fama–French factors. As a result, actively managed mutual funds with positive factor-related past returns accumulate excessive AUM, which leads to significant negative future return performance. I show that this is likely due to the effect of larger trade sizes on price impact and execution costs. In this sense, these funds receive more assets than can be justified by their active management skill. In addition to the fund-level effects, I find that the uninformed factor-return-driven flows have significant power in predicting style-level returns in the future.
Several papers document that the aggregate actively managed equity mutual fund portfolio earns significantly negative benchmark-adjusted returns after fees. I show that the subset of funds with positive factor-related past returns accounts for the negative aggregate return performance of all active equity mutual funds.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
article.Appendix A: Additional Results:
In this section, I present results of several additional exercises. First, I examine whether the findings are robust to excluding the crisis periods or to excluding funds with extreme past returns. Second, I examine the CRSP-classified cap-based funds and style-based funds separately. Third, I verify robustness to using alternative estimation windows (three years or five years) in estimating factor-related returns. Fourth, I use the FFC four-factor model to estimate factor-related average returns. And fifth, I extend SectionIIIby comparing future return performance of funds with different prior FRRs after controlling for both fund AUM and factor-adjusted expected returns simultaneously or for DVA. I find that the results in the main text are robust to each of these alternative specifications.
In this section, I verify robustness of the results. In the first exercise, I exclude NBER recessions from the sample. In the second exercise, I exclude the bottom 5% and top 5% funds by past returns to avoid funds with extreme past performance. In the third and fourth exercises, I focus on the subset of funds classified by CRSP as cap-based funds and as style-based funds, respectively. Results of these four exercises are reported in Panels A, B, C, and D of TableAI. As one can see, the conclusions drawn in SectionsAandBcontinue to hold.
Results of the four exercises are reported in Panels A, B, C, and D of TableAII, respectively. As one can see, the patterns in SectionsAandBare not changed using these alternative specifications.
In SectionIII, I compare future return performance for funds with different prior FRRs, while controlling for either fund AUM or factor-adjusted expected return. Here, I control for DVA, which Berk and van Binsbergen (2015) argue is a proxy for managerial skill.
In this section, I estimate the impact of past FRRs on future return performance by controlling for AUM and “real” alpha simultaneously. To this end, for each calendar year of data, I double sort all funds into nine (3×3)portfolios by fund AUM and by the past-four-year factor-adjusted expected return in (1). I then split each portfolio into three subgroups depending on the sample distribution of factor-related average returns over the past four years. I measure the future performance of each fund group as in SectionsAandB.
TableAIIIreports the differences in future performance between the top-tercile-FRR funds and the low-tercile-FRR funds for each portfolio. Clearly, the high-FRR funds continue to underperform the low-FRR funds after controlling for both fund size and prior “real” alpha.
In this section, I provide additional evidence that supports the role of trading costs in driving the negative return performance of oversized funds. Early work
such as Chen et al. (2004), Yan (2008), and Edelen, Evans, and Kadlec (2013) shows that small-cap funds and growth-style funds tend to observe higher average trading costs and high variation in trading costs. Thus, if fund flows associated with FRRs, through the scale effect, explain the negative future performance of funds with positive prior FRRs, we should observe similar patterns across styles. To test this hypothesis, I split the top-tercile funds by past FRRs into two groups: CRSP-classified small-cap funds or growth-style funds on the one hand and the remaining funds on the other. I then sort each of the two groups into three subgroups based on the proportional trading costs in (9) and I examine their future performance.
TableAIVreports the results. In the first row of Panel A, one can see that among “oversized” funds, small-cap funds and growth funds do indeed have more negative average abnormal returns. When comparing the top-trading-cost subgroup and the bottom-trading-cost subgroup (third row of Panel A), the small-cap funds and growth funds among the oversized funds also have larger variation in returns. These cross-style patterns are consistent with the characterization of trading costs across styles in the literature.
article.Appendix B: Supplementary Results to Sec:
In this section, I first provide more details on the estimation of flow-induced trading at the style level. I then provide additional results that are supplementary to SectionIV.B.
To measure flow-induced trading, I merge the Thomson Reuters CDA/Spectrum database with the CRSP mutual fund database. Mutual funds' holding data come from the CDA-Spectrum database. I also obtain stock-level data from CRSP and Compustat.
At the start of the sample period, my sample covers 54% of all CRSP common stocks, which together represent 95% of the h of the market. This is because mutual funds tend to avoid stocks with tiny capitalization (Frazzini and Lamont (2008)). The sample coverage rises steadily as the relative size of the mutual fund sector grows substantially over time. At the end of the sample period, my sample covers 95% of the number of stocks. On average, the sample contains 78% of the number of CRSP common stocks and 98% of the market capitalization.
In this section, I first show that the results in TableVare robust to excluding NBER recessions. The results are reported in TableBI. I next sort the 27 styles into finer groups (nine groups). I report the future return performance of the top and bottom three groups in TableBII.
I use a portfolio approach to establish a negative relationship between flow-induced trading and future performance at the style level. In TableBIII, I also estimate a panel regression to control for fixed effects that might be correlated with style returns. In particular, I regress style returns over the next year on prior flow-induced trading in (10). As one can see, higher past flow-induced trading significantly predicts lower future style returns. On average, a one-standard-deviation increase in FIT leads to a 5% decrease in style returns over the next year. TableBIIIalso reports regression results based on past 12-quarter and past 16-quarter flow-induced trading, respectively. The negative relationship between past flow-induced trading and future style returns is robust to these alternative windows.
Above, I form style portfolios based on market capitalization, book-to-market ratio, and prior one-year return. In this section, I use only size and book-to-market ratio to classify styles. In particular, I form3×3styles each year based on market capitalization and book-to-market ratio. I sort these styles by past cumulative flow-induced trading in (10) and I study the future performance of the top and bottom styles. TableBIVreports results of an exercise that is similar to TableV. Despite the magnitude being smaller, among the nine size-BM styles the most “crowded” style still significantly underperforms the least “crowded” style at the 1% level.
TableBVreports results of an exercise similar to that in TableVI. I continue to find that funds with high past FRRs are positively exposed to the top-FIT style and negatively exposed to the bottom-FIT style. Funds with negative past FRRs show the opposite, while funds with middle-tercile past FRRs show no relation to these extreme-FIT styles.
